words.shortDefinition: Spending disproportionate time on trivial decisions while ignoring important ones.
words.simpleDefinition: The tendency to focus excessive attention on minor, easy-to-grasp details — like button colors — while avoiding complex, critical architectural decisions. It occurs because trivial things are easier for everyone to have strong opinions about.
words.longDefinition: This productivity anti-pattern occurs when teams spend excessive time on minor, easy-to-grasp details—like naming conventions or UI colors—while avoiding complex, critical architectural decisions. It happens because everyone feels qualified to weigh in on trivial matters, whereas technical depth is required for core issues. High-performing teams mitigate this by setting time limits on minor debates and prioritizing high-impact technical debt.
words.connector: Think of bikeshedding like spending an hour debating pizza toppings for a meeting while ignoring that the building is on fire.
words.techContext: Invoked when teams spend too long debating minor issues like code formatting or naming conventions instead of addressing architecture or performance concerns.
words.exampleSentence: We spent two hours bikeshedding the button color instead of discussing the database migration strategy.
words.etymology: from Parkinson's Law of Triviality (1957) — hypothetical committee debates bikeshed color endlessly.

words.shortDefinition: A chain of seemingly unrelated tasks that must be done before accomplishing the original goal.
words.simpleDefinition: Getting trapped in a series of prerequisites — you wanted to add a simple feature, but now you're upgrading Node, fixing broken tests, and updating CI configurations. It describes the frustrating side-tasks that consume engineering hours.
words.longDefinition: Yak shaving describes a series of seemingly unrelated tasks required to reach a specific goal. You might start by wanting to fix a bug, but realize you need a library update, which requires a newer Node version, which breaks your CI environment. It highlights the hidden complexity of modern development stacks where a simple change can trigger a recursive chain of prerequisites that consume entire days of engineering effort.
words.connector: Think of yak shaving like wanting to make toast but realizing you need bread, but your car needs gas, and now you're at the gas station.
words.techContext: Common when setting up development environments or untangling technical debt. Recognizing yak shaving helps teams question whether all steps are truly necessary.
words.exampleSentence: I just wanted to add a button, but three hours of yak shaving later I'm upgrading Node, fixing tests, and updating CI config.
words.etymology: coined by Carlin Vieri at MIT (2000) inspired by Ren & Stimpy's 'Yak Shaving Day' episode.

codeExample.code:
# Goal: deploy new feature
# But first:
npm update    # yak shave 1
fix tests     # yak shave 2
upgrade CI    # yak shave 3
# ... still haven't deployed the feature

words.shortDefinition: Explaining your code line-by-line to an inanimate object to find bugs.
words.simpleDefinition: The practice of explaining your code line-by-line out loud to an inanimate object — like a rubber duck. This forcing function often reveals hidden bugs as you're obliged to articulate your logical assumptions clearly.
words.longDefinition: Popularized by 'The Pragmatic Programmer,' this technique involves explaining your code line-by-line to an inanimate object. By forcing yourself to articulate the logic out loud, you frequently uncover flaws in your assumptions that remained hidden during silent internal thought. This process triggers a shift from 'writing' mode to 'teaching' mode, which is a powerful psychological tool for identifying logical gaps and off-by-one errors.
words.connector: Think of rubber duck debugging like explaining directions — you often realize you took a wrong turn only when you say it out loud.
words.techContext: A surprisingly effective debugging technique. Many developers find bugs while writing a question for Stack Overflow before ever posting it.
words.exampleSentence: I spent an hour rubber duck debugging with my coworker's plant and found the off-by-one error immediately.
words.etymology: popularized by Hunt and Thomas in The Pragmatic Programmer (1999).

words.shortDefinition: Using your own product internally to identify bugs and usability issues before customers do.
words.simpleDefinition: The practice of using your own software internally before releasing it to customers — 'eating your own dog food.' It helps identify real-world bugs and usability issues while building empathy for the user's daily experience.
words.longDefinition: Formally known as 'eating your own dog food,' this practice involves using your own software in real-world scenarios before it reaches customers. By relying on their own tools for daily operations, engineers gain first-hand experience of the product's friction points, bugs, and performance issues. This builds deep empathy for the user base and ensures that critical defects are caught in the context of actual workflows rather than isolated test environments.
words.connector: Think of dogfooding like chefs tasting their own food in the kitchen — it allows you to catch seasoning problems before serving guests.
words.techContext: Internal dogfooding often catches bugs that QA misses. Also validates whether the product solves real problems.
words.exampleSentence: We started dogfooding the new API and immediately found three edge cases our test suite missed.
words.etymology: from 1970s Alpo dog food commercials where the president ate the product; popularized in tech by Microsoft in 1980s.

codeExample.code:
# Dogfooding example:
# Use your own CI/CD tool to deploy itself
# Use your own monitoring tool to monitor itself
# Ship internal teams to production first

words.shortDefinition: Number of team members who can leave before a project faces critical failure.
words.simpleDefinition: A measurement of knowledge concentration risk — representing how many people could disappear tomorrow before the project fails. A bus factor of one means only one specific person understands a critical system.
words.longDefinition: The bus factor measures the risk associated with knowledge silos within a team. If a critical system is understood by only one person, the project's 'bus factor' is one, making it highly vulnerable to that person's absence. Mature engineering cultures increase this number through rigorous documentation, pair programming, and cross-training, ensuring that no single individual becomes a single point of failure for the entire organization's technical continuity.
words.connector: Think of bus factor like backup keys — if only one person has the keys and they lose them, everyone is locked out.
words.techContext: Low bus factor signals fragility. When key engineers leave, teams scramble to understand their systems. Mitigate via knowledge sharing and docs.
words.exampleSentence: The auth system has a bus factor of one — only Sarah knows how it works, and she's on vacation.
words.etymology: morbid software engineering term from 1990s; sometimes euphemized as 'truck number' or 'lottery factor.'

codeExample.code:
# Bus factor assessment:
auth_service:
  owners: [sarah]
  bus_factor: 1  # ⚠️ risky

payment_service:
  owners: [alex, jordan, pat]
  bus_factor: 3  # ✅ safer

words.shortDefinition: Persistent self-doubt about one's abilities despite evidence of competence.
words.simpleDefinition: The psychological pattern of doubting your accomplishments and fearing you'll be exposed as a 'fraud' — despite evidence of competence. It is extremely common among developers who feel overwhelmed by the vast amount of knowledge.
words.longDefinition: This psychological phenomenon involves persistent self-doubt and a fear of being exposed as a fraud, despite external evidence of competence and success. It is prevalent in technology due to the rapid pace of change and the impossibility of knowing every tool in the stack. Engineering teams combat this by fostering cultures of psychological safety, normalizing the admission of 'I don't know,' and focusing on growth mindset rather than innate genius.
words.connector: Think of imposter syndrome like winning an award but being convinced the committee made a mistake and will eventually take it back.
words.techContext: Affects developers at all levels. Senior engineers often feel it more acutely because they're expected to know everything. Combat it by sharing struggles and celebrating learning.
words.exampleSentence: Even after shipping three major features, I had imposter syndrome during the promotion panel — I felt like I'd just gotten lucky.
words.etymology: coined by psychologists Clance and Imes in 1978; widely discussed in tech culture since 2010s.

codeExample.code:
# Common imposter syndrome thoughts:
# "Everyone else knows what they're doing"
# "I just Googled the solution — does that count?"
# "This feature worked but I don't know why"
# Reality: everyone feels this way sometimes.

words.shortDefinition: Two developers working together at one workstation, one typing while the other reviews in real-time.
words.simpleDefinition: Two developers working together at one workstation — one person (the driver) types while the other (the navigator) reviews each line in real-time. It improves code quality and effectively shares knowledge across the engineering team.
words.longDefinition: In this collaborative practice, two developers work at a single workstation: the 'driver' writes the code while the 'navigator' reviews it in real-time. This dynamic ensures that every line of code is reviewed as it is written, catching errors instantly and facilitating continuous knowledge transfer. While it appears to double the cost of development, it often reduces long-term maintenance by producing higher-quality code and preventing knowledge silos from forming.
words.connector: Think of pair programming like rally racing — one person drives the car while the other navigates the map and watches for hazards.
words.techContext: Effective for onboarding, complex problems, and knowledge transfer. Trade-off: doubles person-hours but reduces review cycles and debugging time.
words.exampleSentence: We pair programmed the payment integration — the navigator caught three potential race conditions before we even ran the code.
words.etymology: formalized in Extreme Programming (1996) by Kent Beck; practiced informally since earlier.

codeExample.code:
# Pair programming session:
# Driver: writes code, tactical focus
# Navigator: reviews, strategic thinking
# Switch roles every 20-30 minutes
# Result: fewer bugs, shared understanding

words.shortDefinition: Systematic examination of code changes by peers before merging to catch issues and share knowledge.
words.simpleDefinition: The process of having teammates examine your code changes before they're merged into the shared mainline. It acts as a critical quality gate — catching bugs, sharing knowledge, and ensuring consistent coding standards across the team.
words.longDefinition: Code review is a systematic peer examination of source code changes before they are merged into the main codebase. It serves as a primary quality gate, catching logic errors, security vulnerabilities, and stylistic inconsistencies. Beyond bug hunting, reviews are essential for knowledge sharing, ensuring that multiple team members understand new changes and that the code adheres to established architectural patterns. Effective reviews provide constructive feedback that improves both the code and the developer.
words.connector: Think of code review like an editor proofreading your article — a fresh pair of eyes catches mistakes you've been staring past.
words.techContext: Good reviews are constructive, specific, and timely. Bad reviews nitpick style while missing logic errors. Many teams require 1-2 approvals before merge.
words.exampleSentence: The code review caught a SQL injection vulnerability I'd missed — the reviewer noticed we weren't sanitizing user input.
words.etymology: formal code review dates to 1970s IBM; GitHub pull requests (2008) democratized the practice.

codeExample.code:
## Code review checklist:
- [ ] Logic correct?
- [ ] Tests added?
- [ ] Security issues?
- [ ] Readable?
- [ ] Follows conventions?

words.shortDefinition: A new software project with no existing code, constraints, or legacy decisions to work around.
words.simpleDefinition: Starting fresh — no existing codebase, no migration baggage, no 'why is this here?' moments. Build it the way you'd build it if you could choose anything.
words.longDefinition: Greenfield work is what every engineer thinks they want and many regret picking. Without legacy constraints you also lack proven design choices and battle-tested edge cases. Greenfield projects often re-discover problems the old system solved years ago — silently.
words.techContext: Common pattern: a small team is tasked with greenfielding a 'modern replacement' for a 10-year-old system. They ship faster initially, then spend years rebuilding the niche features and integrations the old system accumulated.
words.exampleSentence: It's a greenfield rewrite — we get to pick the stack, but we also have to handle the migration from the old data model.
words.etymology: From real-estate development — building on undeveloped 'green' land vs. brownfield (already developed).
words.connector: Like opening a brand-new file — empty, full of possibility, no one's accidentally introduced bugs yet.

codeExample.code:
$ ls
# (empty)

$ git init
Initialized empty Git repository

$ # six months later: re-implementing CSV import that the legacy system shipped in 2015

words.shortDefinition: Repetitive, manual operational work that scales linearly with system growth and produces no lasting value.
words.simpleDefinition: The grindy ops work — restarting flaky services, deploying with hand-edits, copy-pasting between dashboards — that eats your week without making the system better.
words.longDefinition: Toil is Google SRE's term for the operational tax that scales with the system. Onboarding a service by editing five YAML files by hand? Toil. Manually clearing a cache after every deploy? Toil. The SRE answer is to invest in automation: an SRE team aims to spend ≤50% of time on toil so the rest is engineering.
words.techContext: Track toil hours weekly. When toil exceeds 50% of any engineer's time, it's a forcing function — automate, redesign, or kill the offending workflow before burnout.
words.exampleSentence: Half the team's week is toil — manual deploys, ticket triage, runbook execution. We need to invest a sprint in automation.
words.etymology: From Old French toiler — to struggle or labor.
words.connector: Like technical debt's operational sibling — the interest you pay every week in human time.

codeExample.code:
# Toil example: every deploy needs these manual steps
ssh prod-1 'systemctl restart svc' && \
ssh prod-2 'systemctl restart svc' && \
ssh prod-3 'systemctl restart svc' && \
open "https://dashboard/svc?check=after-deploy" && \
echo "remember to clear the CDN cache!"

# Less toil:
kubectl rollout restart deploy/svc

words.shortDefinition: Fixed time box where a team commits to delivering specific work items.
words.simpleDefinition: A fixed time box — typically one or two weeks — during which a development team commits to delivering a specific set of work. It creates a predictable heartbeat for the team, focusing effort and reducing work-in-progress.
words.longDefinition: A sprint is a fixed duration of time—usually one to four weeks—during which a development team works to complete a designated set of tasks from the backlog. This structure provides a predictable rhythm for delivery and allows for regular course correction based on stakeholder feedback. By limiting the time frame, teams can focus on immediate priorities, manage their capacity effectively, and maintain a steady velocity throughout the project's lifecycle.
words.connector: Think of a sprint like a single chapter in a book — it is a small, manageable chunk with a clear beginning, middle, and end.
words.techContext: Sprints force regular delivery and reduce work-in-progress. Critics argue arbitrary time boxes can lead to rushed work or artificial deadlines.
words.exampleSentence: Our two-week sprints start Monday with planning, end Friday with demo and retro, then we immediately plan the next sprint.
words.etymology: term adopted from Scrum methodology (1990s); implies short, focused burst of effort.

codeExample.code:
## Sprint 42 (Jan 15-26):
- Plan auth redesign
- Implement OAuth flow
- Add MFA support
- Write migration runbook

Capacity: 80 story points
Actual: 72 points completed

words.shortDefinition: A brief daily meeting where team members share progress, plans, and blockers.
words.simpleDefinition: A brief, time-boxed daily meeting where team members synchronize activities and surface blockers. Each member typically answers what they did yesterday, what they'll do today, and if anything is preventing their progress. Keeps the team aligned.
words.longDefinition: The daily standup is a brief, time-boxed synchronization meeting where team members share their progress, immediate plans, and any blockers. By keeping the meeting short—often literally standing to encourage brevity—teams can quickly identify dependencies and obstacles without the overhead of a long status meeting. It is a tactical tool for realignment, ensuring that the entire team is aware of the current state of the sprint and can offer immediate help to unblock others.
words.connector: Think of standup like a sports team huddle — a quick sync on the immediate plan so everyone knows what's happening today.
words.techContext: Good standups are concise and action-oriented. Bad standups become status reports to managers. Remote teams often do async standups via Slack.
words.exampleSentence: In standup I mentioned the CI was broken, and Jordan immediately offered to pair on fixing it after the meeting.
words.etymology: from standing during the meeting to keep it brief; formalized in agile methodologies (1990s-2000s).

codeExample.code:
## Standup format:
**Alex:** Finished user auth, starting API integration. Blocked on API docs.
**Jordan:** Reviewed 3 PRs, deploying to staging today. No blockers.
**Pat:** Debugging payment issue. Need help from platform team.

words.shortDefinition: Meeting where teams analyze their past performance to improve future workflows.
words.simpleDefinition: A recurring meeting where the team reflects on their collaborative process — identifying what went well, what failed, and defining concrete experiments for improvement. It is a cornerstone of the agile 'inspect and adapt' philosophy.
words.longDefinition: The retrospective occurs at the end of every sprint, providing a dedicated space for the team to reflect on their collaborative process. Participants identify what worked well, what caused friction, and what should be changed in the next cycle. This 'inspect and adapt' loop is fundamental to continuous improvement, allowing teams to incrementally optimize their communication, tooling, and technical practices based on empirical evidence from their actual working experience over the previous weeks.
words.connector: Think of retrospectives like watching game film after a match — you analyze what went right and wrong to play better next time.
words.techContext: Retros drive continuous improvement. Common formats: Start/Stop/Continue, Mad/Sad/Glad, or Timeline. Remote teams use tools like Retrium or Miro.
words.exampleSentence: In the retrospective we decided to stop scheduling meetings during focus hours and start using mob programming for complex features.
words.etymology: from Latin 'retrospectare' (to look back); formalized in agile as regular reflection practice.

codeExample.code:
## Sprint 42 Retro:
**What went well:** Shipped auth v2 ahead of schedule
**What didn't:** Too many meetings, lost focus time
**Action items:**
- [ ] Block 9-11am as no-meeting time
- [ ] Reduce standup to 10 minutes

words.shortDefinition: The amount of work a team completes per sprint, measured in story points or tasks.
words.simpleDefinition: An agile metric representing the average amount of work a team completes during a single sprint — typically measured in story points. It is used exclusively for long-term capacity planning and forecasting delivery dates.
words.longDefinition: Velocity is a metric that tracks the average amount of work a team completes during a sprint, typically measured in story points. It is primarily used for capacity planning and forecasting, helping teams estimate how long it will take to complete a backlog of features. It is critical to remember that velocity is a tool for planning, not a measure of individual performance, as 'points' are subjective and vary significantly between different engineering teams.
words.connector: Think of velocity like your car's average speed — it helps you estimate arrival time, but speeding up doesn't mean you're a better driver.
words.techContext: Velocity is a planning tool, not a performance metric. Comparing velocity across teams is meaningless — each team's points are calibrated differently.
words.exampleSentence: Our velocity averages 50 points per sprint, so we'll need four sprints to complete this 200-point epic.
words.etymology: borrowed from physics; applied to agile as 'speed of work delivery' in 1990s Scrum practices.

codeExample.code:
# Velocity tracking:
Sprint_40: 48 points
Sprint_41: 52 points
Sprint_42: 45 points
Average:   48 points

# Plan next sprint: 50 points (don't overcommit)

words.shortDefinition: Uncontrolled expansion of a project's requirements after work has begun.
words.simpleDefinition: The uncontrolled expansion of project requirements after development has started — typically occurring when new features are added without adjusting timelines or resources. It is a major cause of project delays and team burnout.
words.longDefinition: Scope creep refers to the gradual, uncontrolled expansion of project requirements without corresponding adjustments to time, budget, or resources. This often occurs when stakeholders add 'small' features mid-development that cumulatively delay the final delivery. Without rigorous change management and a clear definition of the Minimum Viable Product, scope creep can lead to bloated software, missed deadlines, and significant team burnout as engineers struggle to hit moving targets while maintaining code quality.
words.connector: Think of scope creep like packing for a weekend trip that somehow becomes three suitcases and a carry-on as you keep adding 'essentials'.
words.techContext: Common symptom: 'while we're at it, could we also...' Agile mitigates scope creep via fixed sprints and prioritized backlogs.
words.exampleSentence: The payment feature suffered massive scope creep — what started as Stripe integration became a full fraud detection system.
words.etymology: project management term from 1990s; 'creep' implies slow, unnoticed expansion.

codeExample.code:
## Original scope:
- Integrate Stripe
- Handle successful payments

## Scope creep:
- Add PayPal, Apple Pay, Google Pay
- Implement fraud detection
- Build admin dashboard
- Export accounting reports
# ... now 6 weeks behind schedule

words.shortDefinition: A toggle that enables or disables functionality without deploying new code.
words.simpleDefinition: A logical toggle enabling or disabling application functionality at runtime — without requiring a code redeployment. Feature flags facilitate safe gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and instant emergency 'kill switches' for buggy new features.
words.longDefinition: Feature flags are conditional branches in code that allow developers to toggle functionality on or off at runtime without redeploying the application. This technique enables trunk-based development, gradual rollouts to specific user segments, and A/B testing in production environments. By decoupling deployment from release, teams can ship code more frequently and mitigate risk by using flags as 'kill switches' that instantly disable buggy new features if performance issues are detected after launch.
words.connector: Think of feature flags like a light switch — you can install the wiring (deploy code) today but keep the room dark until you're ready.
words.techContext: Flags let you deploy incomplete features, test in production safely, and roll back instantly. Accumulating stale flags creates tech debt — clean them up after rollout.
words.exampleSentence: We deployed the new checkout behind a feature flag set to 10% of users, monitored errors for a day, then rolled it out to everyone.
words.etymology: emerged in continuous delivery practices (2000s-2010s); popularized by tools like LaunchDarkly.

words.shortDefinition: Minimum Viable Product - the smallest version that delivers value and enables learning.
words.simpleDefinition: Minimum Viable Product — the smallest, most functional version of a product that satisfies early customers and provides feedback for future development. It is a learning tool designed to validate core business assumptions with minimal engineering effort.
words.longDefinition: A Minimum Viable Product is the most basic version of a new product that allows a team to collect the maximum amount of validated learning about customers with the least effort. It must be functional enough to solve a core problem for early adopters, providing empirical data to guide future development. The goal is to avoid building features that nobody wants, ensuring that engineering resources are focused on high-value requirements that have been validated by real-world usage.
words.connector: Think of an MVP like a skateboard — it's not a car, but it gets you moving and teaches you what transportation users actually need.
words.techContext: Common mistake: confusing MVP with 'cheapest possible version.' A good MVP solves one problem well rather than many problems poorly.
words.exampleSentence: Our MVP was just Stripe payments and email receipts — we added invoicing, refunds, and reporting based on user feedback.
words.etymology: popularized by Eric Ries in The Lean Startup (2011); concept from agile and lean methodologies.

codeExample.code:
## E-commerce MVP:
✅ Product catalog
✅ Shopping cart
✅ Stripe checkout
✅ Email confirmation

❌ Wishlist (v2)
❌ Reviews (v2)
❌ Recommendations (v3)

words.shortDefinition: Proof of Concept - a small experiment to validate technical feasibility.
words.simpleDefinition: Proof of Concept — a small, non-production experiment designed to validate the technical feasibility of a specific idea or technology. POCs are intended to be throwaway code that answers the critical question: 'can we build this?'.
words.longDefinition: A Proof of Concept is a focused, often throwaway implementation designed to verify that a specific technical idea or method is feasible. Unlike an MVP, a POC is not intended for production use or end-users; its primary purpose is to de-risk a project by answering critical technical questions early in the lifecycle. By exploring complex integrations or new technologies in isolation, teams can make informed decisions about whether to commit significant resources to a particular architectural direction.
words.connector: Think of a POC like a scientist's bench experiment — you're testing if the chemical reaction works before building a giant factory.
words.techContext: Good POCs are time-boxed (1-3 days) and focus on the riskiest assumption. Bad POCs become production code because 'it already works.'
words.exampleSentence: We built a POC to see if Rust could handle our throughput requirements — it worked, so we rewrote the service properly.
words.etymology: project management term adopted by engineering; formalized in technology adoption processes.

codeExample.code:
# POC goal: Can WebAssembly improve performance?
# Time box: 2 days
# Success criteria: 2x speedup on benchmark
# Result: Yes → proceed with full implementation
# POC code: DELETE, start fresh with proper architecture

words.shortDefinition: Software work that must integrate with, refactor, or extend an existing codebase or system.
words.simpleDefinition: Working in an existing codebase — there are constraints, conventions, surprise dependencies, and a graveyard of decisions you have to respect.
words.longDefinition: Brownfield is the honest term for most software work. You inherit a working system with users, integrations, and a long tail of edge cases. Adding features means understanding what's there before changing it. The 'rewrite from scratch' urge is usually the wrong instinct — Chesterton's Fence applies.
words.techContext: Brownfield work demands different skills than greenfield: reading code, mapping dependencies, writing tests around legacy logic before refactoring. Strangler Fig pattern is the canonical brownfield migration approach.
words.exampleSentence: It's a brownfield project — we're migrating the auth flow without touching the rest of the monolith yet.
words.etymology: From real-estate development — building on previously developed land vs. greenfield (untouched).
words.connector: Like editing an existing file vs. creating a new one — most of your effort goes into not breaking what's already there.

codeExample.code:
$ git log --pretty=oneline | wc -l
14823

$ find . -name "*.java" | xargs wc -l | tail -1
  427891 total

$ # week 1: reading. week 4: still reading. week 8: ship a tiny fix.

words.shortDefinition: A bug or issue severe enough to halt a release until fixed.
words.simpleDefinition: A defect so bad it blocks shipping — security vuln, data loss, broken core flow. Anything that crashes the launch.
words.longDefinition: 'Stop-ship' is the trigger phrase that pauses a release. Calling it is a serious move — the release plan, comms, downstream teams all unwind. Called too often, it loses meaning; called too rarely, you ship things you regret. Healthy teams have a clear bar.
words.techContext: Common stop-ship triggers: regression in critical user flow, security vulnerability of CVSS ≥ 7, data corruption, accessibility regression that blocks a known-affected user group. Anything else gets a hot-fix or waits.
words.exampleSentence: The login regression is stop-ship — pull the release, hot-fix, re-tag.
words.etymology: From manufacturing — quality issues severe enough to halt the production line before shipping.
words.connector: Like a CI build going red on `main` — everything stops until it's green again.

codeExample.code:
release/v2.4 status:
  ✓ unit tests       passed
  ✓ integration      passed
  ⨯ e2e: login flow  FAILED on iOS 17

>> stop-ship: hold release, hotfix, re-cut

words.shortDefinition: When git cannot automatically combine changes because two branches modified the same lines.
words.simpleDefinition: Technical collision occurring when two independent branches modify the same line of code in diverging ways — preventing automated integration. It requires a developer to manually review both versions and resolve the discrepancy into a final coherent state.
words.longDefinition: A merge conflict occurs when a version control system like Git is unable to automatically reconcile differences between two branches, typically because the same line of code was modified in both. This requires a developer to manually intervene, analyze both sets of changes, and decide how to combine them correctly. Frequent or large conflicts often signal a lack of communication within the team or that feature branches have lived too long without being integrated into the main branch.
words.connector: Think of a merge conflict like two people trying to enter a narrow doorway at once — someone has to pause and coordinate.
words.techContext: Minimize conflicts via small PRs, trunk-based development, and frequent pulls from main. Some conflicts are unavoidable and healthy.
words.exampleSentence: I had a merge conflict because both my branch and main had refactored the same auth function — I manually combined the changes.
words.etymology: term from version control systems; formalized with distributed VCS like git (2005).

codeExample.code:
<<<<<<< HEAD
function authenticate(user: User) {
  return jwt.sign(user.id);
=======
function authenticate(user: User) {
  return oauth.authorize(user);
>>>>>>> feature-branch

// Resolve manually by choosing or combining

words.shortDefinition: Pull Request - a proposal to merge code changes from one branch into another.
words.simpleDefinition: A formal proposal to merge code changes from a feature branch into the shared mainline — providing a central hub for peer review and discussion. It acts as the primary quality gate in modern collaborative development workflows.
words.longDefinition: A Pull Request (PR) is a formal method of submitting code changes to a repository for review and integration. It provides a central platform where team members can discuss the implementation, run automated tests, and suggest improvements before the code is merged into the master branch. This process is essential for maintaining code quality, ensuring that multiple developers understand new changes, and preventing buggy or poorly architected code from reaching the production environment.
words.connector: Think of a PR like submitting a manuscript to an editor — you've finished your draft, but you wait for feedback before it's published.
words.techContext: Good PRs are focused, well-described, and appropriately sized (<400 lines). Bad PRs dump thousands of lines with no context.
words.exampleSentence: I opened a PR for the auth refactor, tagged two reviewers, and linked the Jira ticket in the description.
words.etymology: coined by GitHub (2008) for their git collaboration workflow; now ubiquitous in software development.

codeExample.code:
## PR #423: Add OAuth support

### Changes:
- Implemented OAuth flow
- Added login button
- Updated tests

### Testing:
- Manual: tested Google & GitHub login
- Automated: 15 new tests passing

Closes #401

words.shortDefinition: Rewriting commit history by moving commits to a new base, creating a linear history.
words.simpleDefinition: The git process of replaying your local commits on top of the latest version of another branch. It rewrites project history to create a clean, linear timeline without the 'clutter' of merge commits.
words.longDefinition: Rebasing is a Git operation that moves or combines a sequence of commits to a new base commit. Unlike merging, which creates a 'merge commit' to join two histories, rebasing rewrites the project history by replaying each local commit on top of the target branch. This results in a cleaner, linear commit history that is easier to navigate. However, it should only be used on local branches, as rebasing public branches can cause significant disruption for other collaborators.
words.connector: Think of rebase like cut-and-pasting your work onto a newer version of the original document, so your changes always appear at the end.
words.techContext: Interactive rebase lets you squash, reorder, or edit commits. Rebase vs merge is a religious debate — both have trade-offs.
words.exampleSentence: Before opening my PR, I rebased my feature branch onto main and squashed my 15 WIP commits into 3 logical commits.
words.etymology: git command introduced by Linus Torvalds (2005); 're-' + 'base' (new foundation for commits).

codeExample.code:
# Rebase feature branch onto main:
git checkout feature
git rebase main  # replays feature commits on latest main

# Interactive rebase (squash commits):
git rebase -i HEAD~5  # edit last 5 commits

words.shortDefinition: A single repository containing multiple projects or services, as opposed to one repo per project.
words.simpleDefinition: Version control strategy of storing multiple distinct projects and services within a single shared repository. It simplifies cross-project code sharing and atomic changes but requires sophisticated build tools to maintain developer productivity at scale.
words.longDefinition: A monorepo is a version control strategy where multiple distinct projects or services are stored within a single repository. This approach simplifies dependency management and enables atomic changes across multiple services, which is particularly useful in microservice architectures. While it requires specialized build tooling like Bazel or Nx to manage scale and maintain fast CI/CD pipelines, a monorepo facilitates easier code sharing and ensures that all projects are always using the most recent versions of internal libraries.
words.connector: Think of a monorepo like one big toolbox for all your projects instead of having separate boxes in different sheds.
words.techContext: Monorepos require good tooling (Bazel, Nx, Turborepo) to remain fast. Not the same as a monolith — you can have microservices in a monorepo.
words.exampleSentence: We moved to a monorepo so updating a shared library didn't require coordinating releases across 10 separate repositories.
words.etymology: portmanteau of 'monolithic' + 'repository'; term emerged in 2010s as companies scaled version control strategies.

codeExample.code:
# Monorepo structure:
my-monorepo/
├── services/
│   ├── api/
│   ├── web/
│   └── worker/
├── packages/
│   ├── shared-ui/
│   └── utils/
└── package.json  # one lockfile, shared deps

words.shortDefinition: A principle: don't remove or change something whose purpose you don't understand.
words.simpleDefinition: Before deleting that weird code, find out why it's there. Someone wrote it for a reason — even if the reason is buried in an old incident.
words.longDefinition: G.K. Chesterton's parable: a fence stands across a road; a reformer wants to tear it down because he sees no use for it. Chesterton's reply: until you know why the fence is there, you have no business removing it. In code: that mysterious retry loop, that magic constant, that 'never delete' file — investigate before deleting.
words.techContext: Common application: a senior engineer wants to delete an `if (legacy_flag)` branch nobody documents. Six months later, an integration partner calls because their batch job is broken. The branch was the fence.
words.exampleSentence: Don't drop the index — Chesterton's Fence. Find out which query depends on it before declaring it dead weight.
words.etymology: From G.K. Chesterton's 1929 book The Thing.
words.connector: Like a `// don't delete this` comment — except the comment is missing and you have to reconstruct the reasoning yourself.
